title: Simple Nightstand
summary: An open-shelf nightstand with a single drawer, built from 1-by boards and a brad nailer. A clean first-drawer project.
description:
This is a deliberately forgiving build — butt joints, glue, and brad nails, no pocket holes or slides required. The whole thing is a pair of shelves held between four built-up legs, with a simple nailed drawer box that rides on wooden guides instead of metal hardware.

The one detail that makes or breaks it is the legs. Each leg is a 1x2 and a 1x3 joined into an L, and the orientation matters: the wider 1x3 faces front and back, the 1x2 faces the sides. Get that backwards and the drawer opening comes out the wrong width and the drawer won't fit. The plan hammers this point for a reason.

Because the drawer runs on wood guides, the payoff is in careful measuring, not expensive parts. Confirm the drawer opening is truly 11" wide and about 11" deep before you build the box, and the drawer drops in like it was meant to.

tools.note: The whole case and drawer are glue-and-brad-nail. A hammer and finish nails work but are slower and more prone to splitting the 1x2s.
Or a miter saw. You just need clean square crosscuts.

materials.name: 1x12, 8 ft
materials.note: Shelves and the front countertop piece.

materials.name: 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Leg pieces, drawer guides, and trim.

materials.name: 1x3, 8 ft
materials.note: Front/back leg pieces and the back countertop piece.

materials.name: 1/2" plywood
materials.note: Drawer box sides and back — a scrap/partial sheet is plenty. A 1x12 can substitute for the sides.

cutList.note: Part B — the side face of each leg.
Part C — the front/back face of each leg.
Part D. If using a 1x12 instead, make the plywood back (E) 1/2" shorter.

steps.title: Build the four legs, watch the orientation
steps.body:
Each leg is a 1x2 glued and nailed to a 1x3 along the long edge to form an L. Make all four.

The orientation is the single most important thing in this build: the 1x3 face goes on the front and back of the nightstand, the 1x2 face on the sides. Mark each leg 3-1/2" up from the bottom while you're at it — that mark sets the shelf height. Get the leg orientation wrong and the drawer will not fit.

steps.title: Attach the bottom shelf
steps.body: Fasten the legs to one shelf so the bottom of the shelf sits flush with the 3-1/2" marks from the previous step. That leaves a 3-1/2" clearance under the nightstand. Keep the 1x3 faces front and back.

steps.title: Attach the top shelf
steps.body: Mark the legs 5-3/4" down from the top on the inside faces, and attach the top shelf there. That 5-3/4" clearance from the top of the shelf to the top of the piece is the drawer box's home.

steps.title: Add the drawer box sides
steps.body: With the case standing, add the plywood side pieces of the drawer box. If you chose to use 1x12 for the sides instead of plywood, remember to make the back piece (E) 1/2" shorter — 13-1/2" x 5-3/4".

steps.title: Add the front and side trim
steps.body: Nail the front trim piece (G) along the bottom edge, keeping its top edge flush with the shelf. Then add the four side trim pieces (H) to the bottom shelf and the bottom of the drawer-box sides.

steps.title: Install the countertop, then verify the opening
steps.body:
Add the back countertop piece (I) with a 1" overhang on each side, then the front countertop piece (J) with a 1" overhang on the sides and front.

Before you build the drawer, measure the inside of the drawer box: it should be exactly 11" wide and about 11" deep (10-1/4" to the bottom shelf, 11" to the side of the legs). Fix any discrepancy now — the drawer is built to these numbers.

steps.title: Build the drawer
steps.body:
Start with the bottom piece (K) and nail the back (L) to it, driving through the back into the bottom. Add a side, nailing through the side into both the bottom and the back; repeat on the other side. Finally nail the face (N) on.

Work on a flat surface and keep everything square as you go — a drawer racked out of square binds in a wood-guided opening.

steps.title: Fit the drawer, then finish
steps.body:
If everything was measured honestly, the drawer slides into the opening on its guides with a clean fit. If it binds, find the tight spot rather than forcing it.

Fill nail holes with wood filler, sand smooth, and finish as you like. Add the knob last.

images.alt: A small open-shelf nightstand with a single drawer and a lower shelf
